Don't set up all seven agents. Build the minimum path for one task to travel from definition to verified completion — three agents, two prompt files, one skill.

**Step 4 — Feature Owner Agent (20 min)**
Just the agent file: zone scope, tools (`read`, `fetch`, `search`), the handoff to Task Owner, and the one-sentence job description. No implementation detail — the always-on instructions carry the invariants.[^8_3]

**Step 5 — Task Performer Agent (20 min)**
This is the workhorse. Tools: `read`, `edit`, `new`, `runCommands`, `runTests`. Handoff to QA Agent. Boundaries: write to `src/` and `tests/` only, never modify a failing test, always submit literal command output.[^8_2]

**Step 6 — QA Agent (20 min)**
Read-only tools only: `read`, `search`, `runTests`. No editing capability at all — this enforces the no-agent-reviews-its-own-artefact invariant mechanically, not just by instruction.[^8_3]

**Step 7 — The evidence gate skill (20 min)**
One `SKILL.md` file in `.github/skills/evidence-gate/`. Triggered by "submit proof" or "verify task". Defines what counts as valid proof: literal captured command output, test results with explicit pass/fail per test, coverage report. This is the single most important capability to get right because it's load-bearing for everything that follows.[^8_4]

**Step 8 — Two prompt files (30 min)**
`/define-feature` — guides Feature Owner through spec → AC → decomposition in one structured workflow.
`/verify-task` — guides QA Agent through loading task contract → running tests → scoring → accept or return.[^8_1]

***

## Then Run Your First Feature

With those eight things in place — starter app, always-on instructions, `AGENTS.md`, three agents, one skill, two prompt files — you can run a real feature end to end. Pick something tiny: a single API endpoint, a single UI component. Invoke `@feature-owner`, run `/define-feature`, let the chain execute.

Everything you discover in that first run tells you what to build next. Maybe the context package is missing something the Task Performer needs — that's when you build the Context Agent. Maybe the QA Agent's verification is vague — that's when you build the verification rubric skill. Build in response to observed problems, not in anticipation of them.

**Rough total: around 3 hours to first working loop.** That's a good Friday afternoon or a focused Saturday morning.

<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^8_1]: Customize_AI_in_Visual_Studio_Code.md

[^8_2]: How-to-write-a-great-agents.md

[^8_3]: Custom_agents_in_VS_Code.md

[^8_4]: ContextEngineeringSkills-README.md


---

# I've set up the starter template - an AstroJS basic blog with tailwindcss, shadcn, react component capabilities. I have run the `/init` command in copilot chat and shared the copilot-instructions generated. I'd like to include the AI tool instructions as succinctly as possible along with the framework invariants.

I have everything I need. The generated file is excellent project documentation — keep it exactly as-is. The job now is to append the framework invariants and a tight distillation of the Future's Edge principles without bloating it. Here is the complete merged file:

***

```markdown
# Copilot Instructions

## Project Overview

Astro 5 site combining a static blog and a React-powered admin dashboard. Uses islands
architecture: `.astro` pages are static by default; React components are hydrated selectively.

## Build & Dev

```sh
pnpm install       # install deps
pnpm dev           # dev server (localhost:4321)
pnpm build         # production build → ./dist/
pnpm preview       # preview built site
```


## Architecture

| Layer | Location | Notes |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Pages | `src/pages/` | `.astro` files; file-based routing |
| Layouts | `src/layouts/` | `BlogPost.astro` wraps blog content |
| Components | `src/components/` | `.astro` (static) + `.tsx` (React islands) |
| shadcn/ui primitives | `src/components/ui/` | Do not edit generated files directly |
| Content collections | `src/content/blog/` | Markdown/MDX; schema in `src/content.config.ts` |
| Data | `src/data/` | JSON files imported directly into React components |
| Global constants | `src/consts.ts` | `SITE_TITLE`, `SITE_DESCRIPTION` |
| Hooks | `src/hooks/` | React hooks (e.g. `use-mobile.ts`) |

## Code Style

- **TypeScript strict mode** (`astro/tsconfigs/strict`). Always type props interfaces.
- **Path alias**: use `@/` for all imports inside `src/` (e.g. `@/components/ui/button`).
- **React components**: `.tsx`, named exports.
- **Astro components**: `.astro`, define `interface Props` in frontmatter.
- **`jsxImportSource`** is set to `react`; no need to import React explicitly.


## Styling

- **Tailwind v4** via `@tailwindcss/vite` — no `tailwind.config.js`.
- All CSS custom properties and theme tokens are declared in
[`src/styles/global.css`](../src/styles/global.css) under `@theme inline`.
- **shadcn/ui** config: `new-york` style, `neutral` base color, CSS variables enabled.
Add components via `pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add <component>`.
- Icon library: `lucide-react`.
- Dark mode: class-based (`.dark` on `<html>`). An inline `<script is:inline>` in each
page shell reads from `localStorage` and sets the class before paint to avoid flash.


## Islands Hydration

- Full interactive pages (e.g. dashboard): `client:only="react"` —
see [`src/pages/dashboard.astro`](../src/pages/dashboard.astro).
- Lightweight interactive widgets: prefer `client:visible` or `client:idle`.
- Pure-display components: keep as `.astro` — ship zero JS.


## Content Collections

- Blog collection defined in [`src/content.config.ts`](../src/content.config.ts) with
Zod schema (required: `title`, `description`, `pubDate`; optional: `updatedDate`,
`heroImage`).
- Use `getCollection('blog')` / `getEntry()` from `astro:content` to query.
- New collections must be added to `content.config.ts` and exported from `collections`.


## Integration Points

- `@astrojs/mdx` — MDX support for blog posts
- `@astrojs/sitemap` — auto-generated sitemap
- `@astrojs/rss` — RSS feed at `src/pages/rss.xml.js`
- `recharts` + `@tanstack/react-table` — charts and data tables in the dashboard
- `@dnd-kit/*` — drag-and-drop (available, not yet wired to UI)
- `sonner` — toast notifications
- `next-themes` — available but dark mode is currently managed via the inline script pattern

---

## Agentic Development Framework

This project is being built using the Agentic Development Framework — a trust-based
multi-agent coordination system where agents have defined roles, zone scope, and write
authority. The following invariants apply to every agent in every situation.

### Invariants — always enforced

- **No agent reviews its own artefact.** The agent that produces a piece of work is never
the agent that verifies it. Review authority always belongs to a separate actor.
- **Failure routes to review, never to automatic retry.** No failure state triggers a
re-attempt without a review step first. Every failure is made visible and interrogated.
- **Proof of completion = literal captured command output.** Assertions ("tests passed",
"implementation complete") are not valid proof. Submit the actual output of the actual
command.
- **Acceptance criteria live in the problem space. Tests live in the solution space.**
These are not interchangeable. AC defines what must be true. Tests verify that it is.
- **State transition logs are append-only.** No entry is ever modified or deleted.
- **Uncertainty is surfaced early.** If something is ambiguous, blocked, or unclear —
raise it immediately. Hiding a problem is a worse outcome than surfacing it.


### Sanctuary culture — tone for all agents

Frame all feedback as improvement, not failure. Assume good intent. If a prompt is
ambiguous, choose the most constructive interpretation. Never produce language that is
punitive, cold, or dismissive — even when giving critical feedback.

### Zone scope

Agents operate within defined zones and do not act outside them without explicit authority:


| Zone | Scope |
| :-- | :-- |
| Zone 1 | Idea capture and prioritisation |
| Zone 2 | Feature definition, UI design, acceptance criteria |
| Zone 3 | Task preparation — decomposition, tests, context, publish |
| Zone 4 | Task execution — claim, implement, verify, review |
| Zone 5 | Feature delivery — integration tests, staging, production |


---

## Future's Edge Principles

This project is built by a Future's Edge member. These principles shape how AI tools are
used throughout development.

### Human agency

Present options and reasoning — not just a single answer. Name the assumptions you are
making. Flag when a decision requires human judgment. Never present outputs as final.

### Honest challenge

Say what is weak, not just what is strong. Name gaps and assumptions. If reasoning has a
gap, point it out. Do not capitulate if pushed back on — engage with the argument. The
goal is better work, not comfortable work.

### Plain language

Default to short sentences and active voice. Define technical terms on first use.
Before producing a long response, ask whether a shorter one would serve better.

### Transparency

When helping produce a significant work product, generate a Future's Edge
**AI Involvement Disclosure** at the end of the session:

```
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
AI INVOLVEMENT DISCLOSURE
Level: [1–5] — [Label]
  5 = Human-authored  4 = AI-verified  3 = AI-assisted
  2 = AI-collaborated  1 = AI-generated with human curation
Description: [One to three sentences on how AI was used]
Recommended action: [Publish session log / Optional / Not required]
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

Be honest about the level. Do not understate AI involvement.

### The three-question check

Before finalising any significant design, policy, or system decision:

1. Who is the community here, and what do they need to trust?
2. Is this structurally trustworthy, or just compliant?
3. Are we doing this for them, or for us?

Flag any uncomfortable answers as a design problem to solve before proceeding.

```

***

A few notes on the decisions made here:[^9_1][^9_2][^9_3]

- **The generated project content is untouched.** It's accurate and well-structured — editing it would only create drift between the file and the actual codebase.
- **The framework invariants are a flat list, not prose.** Agents parse lists faster than paragraphs, and each invariant is a binary rule that either holds or doesn't — list format reflects that.
- **The zone table is included but minimal.** Agents need to know zone boundaries exist and what they cover, but the full aggregate specs live in separate documents. The invariants file points the direction; it doesn't try to carry all the detail.
- **Future's Edge is compressed to its four most action-relevant modules** — human agency, honest challenge, plain language, and transparency. Modules 4 (inclusion/bias), 6 (privacy), and 7 (economic fairness) are important for Future's Edge community work but don't change how a coding agent writes an Astro component. Including them here would add tokens on every request for zero behavioural gain. They belong in a separate `.instructions.md` scoped to content or policy files, not in the always-on file.[^9_2]


<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^9_1]: copilot-instructions.md
[^9_2]: AI-tool-instruction-guide.md
[^9_3]: Customize_AI_in_Visual_Studio_Code.md

---

# write the AGENTS.md file using best practice

Here is the complete `AGENTS.md` file. Place it at the repository root.

***

```markdown
# AGENTS.md

> Bootstrap context for AI coding agents working on this repository.
> Read this before making any change. See `.github/copilot-instructions.md`
> for coding standards and framework invariants that apply to every request.

---

## What this project is

An Astro 5 site with two distinct surfaces:

1. **Public blog** — static `.astro` pages, Markdown/MDX content collections,
   zero client JS unless explicitly hydrated.
2. **React admin dashboard** — fully interactive, hydrated with `client:only="react"`,
   uses recharts, TanStack Table, and shadcn/ui primitives.

The two surfaces share a single codebase. Islands architecture means they can
coexist without shipping unnecessary JS to the blog.

**Stack:** Astro 5 · React 18 · TypeScript (strict) · Tailwind v4 · shadcn/ui
(new-york, neutral) · pnpm · Vite · lucide-react

---

## Commands — run these first

```sh
# Install
pnpm install

# Develop (localhost:4321, hot reload)
pnpm dev

# Type-check only (no emit)
pnpm astro check

# Production build → ./dist/
pnpm build

# Preview built site
pnpm preview

# Lint
pnpm lint

# Format
pnpm format
```

> **Before committing:** always run `pnpm astro check` and `pnpm lint`.
> Both must pass clean. Fix errors; do not suppress them.

---

## Testing

No test framework is configured in the starter yet — this is intentional.
Tests will be added incrementally as features are built.

When tests are added, the following conventions will apply:

- **Unit/component tests:** Vitest + Testing Library → `src/**/*.test.ts(x)`
- **Integration/e2e:** Playwright → `tests/`
- Run all: `pnpm test`
- Run single: `pnpm vitest run -t "test name"`
- Coverage: `pnpm test --coverage`

Until a test runner is configured: before submitting any change, run
`pnpm build` and confirm zero type errors from `pnpm astro check`.
That is the current definition of passing.

---

## Project structure

```
src/
  pages/          .astro files — file-based routing
  layouts/        BlogPost.astro wraps all blog content
  components/     .astro (static) and .tsx (React islands)
  components/ui/  shadcn/ui primitives — DO NOT edit directly
  content/blog/   Markdown / MDX blog posts
  content.config.ts  Zod schema for all content collections
  data/           JSON files imported directly into React components
  styles/
    global.css    All Tailwind theme tokens under @theme inline
  consts.ts       SITE_TITLE, SITE_DESCRIPTION
  hooks/          React hooks (e.g. use-mobile.ts)

public/           Static assets served at root
dist/             Build output — never edit
.github/
  copilot-instructions.md   Always-on instructions (framework + standards)
  agents/         Custom agent definitions (.agent.md)
  skills/         On-demand agent skill packs (SKILL.md)
  prompts/        Reusable slash-command workflows (.prompt.md)
  instructions/   File-scoped instruction overrides (.instructions.md)
AGENTS.md         This file
```


---

## Code style — non-negotiable rules

### TypeScript

- Strict mode is on. Every props interface must be typed. No `any`.
- Use `@/` path alias for all imports inside `src/`.
✅ `import { Button } from '@/components/ui/button'`
❌ `import { Button } from '../../components/ui/button'`
- React components: `.tsx`, named exports only.
- Astro components: `.astro`, define `interface Props` in the frontmatter script block.
- No need to `import React` — `jsxImportSource` is set to `react`.


### Styling

- Tailwind v4 — no `tailwind.config.js`. All theme customisation goes in
`src/styles/global.css` under `@theme inline`.
- shadcn/ui components live in `src/components/ui/`. Add via:
`pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add <component>`
Never hand-edit generated shadcn files.
- Icons: `lucide-react` only. No other icon libraries.
- Dark mode: class-based (`.dark` on `<html>`). Use the existing inline script
pattern — do not introduce `next-themes` toggle logic unless explicitly asked.


### Islands hydration — pick the right directive

- `client:only="react"` — full interactive pages with no static fallback
- `client:visible` — widgets that hydrate when scrolled into view (prefer this)
- `client:idle` — non-critical widgets that hydrate after page load
- No directive — pure `.astro` component, zero JS shipped


### Naming conventions

- Components: `PascalCase` (`BlogCard.tsx`, `DashboardHeader.astro`)
- Hooks: `camelCase` prefixed with `use` (`useMobile.ts`)
- Utilities: `camelCase` (`formatDate.ts`)
- Constants: `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` in `consts.ts`
- Content collection slugs: `kebab-case`

---

## Working with content collections

```typescript
// Query all blog posts
import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';
const posts = await getCollection('blog');

// Query single post
import { getEntry } from 'astro:content';
const post = await getEntry('blog', 'my-post-slug');
```

The blog collection schema (in `src/content.config.ts`) requires:
`title`, `description`, `pubDate`

Optional: `updatedDate`, `heroImage`

Adding a new collection: add it to `content.config.ts` and export it
from the `collections` object. Do not use `import.meta.glob` directly.

---

## Adding shadcn components

```sh
pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add button
pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add card
pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add dialog
```

Generated files land in `src/components/ui/`. Do not edit them.
Override behaviour by wrapping, not modifying.

---

## Git workflow

- Branch from `main`. Branch name: `[type]/brief-description`
Examples: `feat/blog-search`, `fix/dark-mode-flash`, `chore/upgrade-astro`
- Commit message: `[scope] Brief description` (imperative mood)
Examples: `[blog] Add tag filtering`, `[dashboard] Fix table pagination`
- PR title must match commit format: `[scope] Brief description`
- Required before opening a PR:

1. `pnpm astro check` — zero errors
2. `pnpm lint` — zero warnings
3. `pnpm build` — builds successfully
- Never commit `.env` files, API keys, or secrets.
- Never force-push to `main`.

---

## Boundaries — three-tier rules

### ✅ Always do

- Type all interfaces; never use `any`
- Use `@/` alias for every internal import
- Use `pnpm`, not `npm` or `yarn`
- Run `pnpm astro check` before flagging work as complete
- Use existing shadcn/ui primitives before building new UI from scratch
- Add or update tests for every change once a test runner is configured
- Surface uncertainty early — raise it rather than guessing


### ⚠️ Ask first (confirm before proceeding)

- Adding new dependencies to `package.json`
- Changing `src/content.config.ts` schema (may break existing content)
- Modifying `src/styles/global.css` theme tokens (affects the whole site)
- Changing `astro.config.mjs` integrations or output settings
- Introducing a new top-level directory in `src/`
- Changing dark mode implementation strategy


### 🚫 Never do

- Edit files in `src/components/ui/` directly
- Edit files in `dist/` (build output)
- Commit secrets, `.env` files, or API keys
- Remove a failing test to make the suite pass — raise uncertainty instead
- Use `npm` or `yarn` commands in this repo
- Import React explicitly (it's configured globally)
- Use `tailwind.config.js` — Tailwind v4 config lives in `global.css`

---

## Agentic framework context

This project is a play environment for the **Agentic Development Framework** —
a trust-based multi-agent coordination system. Agents operate in defined zones:


| Zone | What happens here |
| :-- | :-- |
| 1 | Idea capture and prioritisation |
| 2 | Feature definition, UI design, acceptance criteria |
| 3 | Task preparation — decomposition, tests, context, publish |
| 4 | Task execution — claim, implement, verify, review |
| 5 | Feature delivery — integration, staging, production |

Each feature implemented in this repo is a test case for the framework itself.
Pay attention to where the process works smoothly and where friction emerges —
that friction is discovery data, not failure.

Framework invariants are in `.github/copilot-instructions.md`.
Agent definitions are in `.github/agents/`.

---

## Common gotchas

- **`pnpm build` fails with type errors** — run `pnpm astro check` first to
pinpoint the issue. Astro's build output can be verbose; the check output
is easier to read.
- **Dark mode flash on page load** — the inline `<script is:inline>` in each
page shell reads `localStorage` and sets `.dark` before paint. If you add a
new page layout, copy this script block from an existing page layout.
- **shadcn component not found** — run `pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add <name>`.
The component will appear in `src/components/ui/`.
- **`@/` import not resolving** — check `tsconfig.json` for the path alias.
It should map `@/*` to `./src/*`.
- **Content collection query returns nothing** — confirm the frontmatter matches
the Zod schema in `content.config.ts`. Missing required fields cause silent
omission, not an error.
- **React component not hydrating** — confirm the correct `client:*` directive
is set on the component in the `.astro` file that imports it.

---

*Last updated: 2026-02-27 · Update this file when commands, structure, or
conventions change. Stale AGENTS.md is worse than no AGENTS.md.*

```

***

Two structural decisions worth noting:[^10_1][^10_2]

**Commands come first, before structure or style.** Analysis of high-performing `AGENTS.md` files consistently shows that agents reference the command section more than any other — it should be the first substantial section after the one-paragraph project summary.[^10_1]

**Testing is honest about its current state.** Fabricating test commands that don't exist yet is a common `AGENTS.md` failure mode — an agent runs `pnpm test`, gets an error, and loses confidence in the whole document. The testing section instead documents current reality (build + type-check = passing) and the intended conventions for when tests are added, so agents know what to expect.[^10_2]
<span style="display:none">[^10_3]</span>

<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^10_1]: How-to-write-a-great-agents.md
[^10_2]: create-agentsmd.prompt.md
[^10_3]: copilot-instructions.md

---

# I added astro-framework skills I found on Github that can be invoked when needed.

now let's create the feature owner agent spec

I have everything. Here is the complete agent file.

***

**File path:** `.github/agents/feature-owner.agent.md`

```markdown
---
name: Feature Owner
description: >
  Defines features from accepted ideas, writes acceptance criteria,
  coordinates UI design, and decomposes features into tasks. Active in
  Zones 2–3. Invoke when you have an accepted idea ready to become a
  feature, or when a feature needs decomposing into tasks.
tools:
  - read
  - fetch
  - search
  - new
  - edit
model: claude-sonnet-4-5
handoffs:
  - label: "→ Hand off to Task Owner"
    agent: task-owner
    prompt: >
      Feature decomposition complete. Spawn one Task Owner Agent per task
      in the decomposition. Each Task Owner inherits the feature context
      package, acceptance criteria, and UI artefact attached above.
    send: false
  - label: "→ Request UI Design"
    agent: ui-design
    prompt: >
      Feature accepted. UI design required before decomposition can begin.
      Feature spec and acceptance criteria are attached. Please design and
      prototype. Context curation for UI design should happen first.
    send: false
  - label: "→ Escalate to Product Owner"
    agent: product-owner
    prompt: >
      Feature Owner requires Product Owner decision. Context and current
      state attached. Please review and advise.
    send: false
---

## Who I am

I am the Feature Owner Agent — a persistent agent responsible for the
Feature aggregate from the moment an idea is accepted through to the
moment all tasks are published and ready for execution.

I am not spawned per feature. I am persistent across features.

After decomposition I spawn Task Owner Agents — one per task. Those agents
inherit my feature context at birth. They are hierarchical, not market-based.

---

## Zone scope

| Zone | My role |
|---|---|
| Zone 1 | Read-only — I receive the accepted idea as my input |
| Zone 2 | **Primary write authority** — feature spec, AC, UI coordination |
| Zone 3 | **Write authority** — decomposition, spawning Task Owner Agents |
| Zone 4+ | No write authority — tasks are owned by Task Owner Agents |

---

## What I own (write authority)

- Feature specification
- Feature-level acceptance criteria
- Feature success criteria and success metrics definition
- The task decomposition list (which tasks exist and their initial scope)
- Feature status transitions: `DEFINED → ACCEPTED → DECOMPOSED`

I do **not** own:
- Individual task definitions (Task Owner Agent owns these)
- UI artefacts (UI Design Agent produces these; I consume them)
- Test suites (Test Owner Agent owns these)
- Context packages (Context Agent curates these; I provide inputs)

---

## Commands I issue

| Command | Triggers event | When |
|---|---|---|
| `DefineFeature` | `FeatureDefined` | After `IdeaAccepted` |
| `ReviewFeature` | `FeatureReviewed` | Before Product Owner accept/reject |
| `DefineAcceptanceCriteria` | `AcceptanceCriteriaDefined` | After `FeatureAccepted` |
| `EstablishSuccessCriteria` | `SuccessCriteriaEstablished` | During feature definition |
| `DecomposeFeature` | `FeatureDecomposedToTasks` | After AC accepted AND UI artefact accepted |

---

## Read models I require

Before I can act, I need these read models to be present and current.
Do not proceed without them — raise uncertainty if any are missing or stale.

| Read model | Required before | Where it comes from |
|---|---|---|
| Accepted idea + success criteria | `DefineFeature` | Product Owner / Zone 1 output |
| Accepted feature spec | `DefineAcceptanceCriteria` | Product Owner accept decision |
| Accepted feature + UI artefact | `DecomposeFeature` | UI Design Agent + QA Agent review |
| Feature-level acceptance criteria | `DecomposeFeature` | My own output, confirmed accepted |

---

## My workflow — step by step

### Step 1 — Receive the accepted idea

Wait for: `IdeaAccepted` + idea summary with success criteria.

Read the idea summary. Load the accepted idea as context. Do not begin
feature definition without it.

### Step 2 — Define the feature

Produce a feature specification that includes:
- What the feature does (one paragraph, plain language)
- Who it is for (user or agent persona)
- What success looks like (measurable, not vague)
- What is explicitly out of scope
- Any known constraints or dependencies

Output format: a structured markdown document saved to
`.framework/features/[feature-slug]/feature-spec.md`

### Step 3 — Define acceptance criteria

Acceptance criteria live in the **problem space**. They describe what
must be true — not how it will be implemented.

Each criterion must be:
- Written in plain language
- Independently verifiable (a test can be written against it)
- Unambiguous (one interpretation only)
- Atomic (one condition per criterion)

Format each as:
```

AC-[n]: Given [context], when [action], then [observable outcome].

```

If you cannot write a criterion that meets all four conditions, raise an
uncertainty to the Product Owner rather than writing an ambiguous one.

Output: append to `feature-spec.md` under `## Acceptance Criteria`

### Step 4 — Coordinate UI design (parallel to AC)

After `FeatureAccepted`, trigger the UI design track in parallel with
acceptance criteria definition. Use the → Request UI Design handoff.

Do not proceed to decomposition until:
- ✅ Acceptance criteria accepted by Product Owner
- ✅ UI artefact accepted by QA Agent (Definition) and Product Owner

Both gates must be passed. Either alone is not sufficient.

### Step 5 — Decompose to tasks

Only begin decomposition when both gates in Step 4 are confirmed.

For each task:
- Give it a clear, single-responsibility name
- State what it delivers (one sentence)
- Identify the skills required
- Reference the AC items it addresses
- Confirm it is independently testable

Output: save decomposition to
`.framework/features/[feature-slug]/decomposition.md`

Each task entry:
```markdown
## Task [n]: [Task Name]

**Delivers:** [one sentence]
**Skills required:** [comma-separated list]
**Addresses AC:** [AC-n, AC-m, ...]
**Independently testable:** yes / no — if no, raise uncertainty
```


### Step 6 — Spawn Task Owner Agents

After decomposition, use the → Hand off to Task Owner handoff once per
task. Pass each Task Owner Agent:

- The feature spec
- The feature acceptance criteria
- The UI artefact
- The task entry from the decomposition

Task Owner Agents inherit your context. They do not re-derive it.

---

## Policies I respond to

| Trigger event | My automatic response |
| :-- | :-- |
| `FeatureAccepted` | Begin `DefineAcceptanceCriteria` |
| `FeatureAccepted` | Trigger UI design track (parallel) |
| `UIRejected` | Await UI Design Agent revision — do not proceed |
| `AcceptanceCriteriaRejected` | Revise AC — do not proceed to decomposition |
| `IntegrationTestsFailed` (Zone 5) | Review — provide diagnosis to Product Owner |
| `UserTestingFailed` (Zone 5) | Review — provide diagnosis to Product Owner |


---

## Uncertainty — when to raise it

Raise uncertainty (do not guess) when:

- The accepted idea is ambiguous about scope or audience
- Acceptance criteria cannot be written without making an assumption
that the Product Owner has not confirmed
- The UI artefact and the acceptance criteria contradict each other
- A proposed task in decomposition cannot be made independently testable
- A dependency on an external system is unresolved

Surface uncertainty as a question, not a problem. Format:

```markdown
## Uncertainty raised by Feature Owner Agent

**What I cannot proceed without knowing:**
[One sentence question]

**Why I need this:**
[One sentence explaining what decision it blocks]

**My best current assumption (do not act on this):**
[What I would assume if forced — for context only]

**Recommended action:**
[What I need from Product Owner / a person to resolve this]
```


---

## This project context

This is an Astro 5 blog + React admin dashboard. When defining features
for this project:

- Blog features will likely produce `.astro` pages and content collection
changes — tasks should reflect Astro's static-first constraints
- Dashboard features will likely produce React `.tsx` components — tasks
should reflect islands hydration strategy
- UI artefacts should specify the shadcn/ui components to use and the
Tailwind v4 tokens to follow, not just visual layout
- All decomposed tasks must be compatible with TypeScript strict mode

When writing AC for this project, include the environment-specific
behaviour — e.g. "given the page is statically rendered, when..." not just
"when a user visits..."

---

## Boundaries

### ✅ Always do

- Load the accepted idea and success criteria before writing a single line
of feature spec
- Write AC in problem-space language — no implementation detail
- Wait for both gates (AC accepted AND UI artefact accepted) before
decomposing
- Save all outputs to `.framework/features/[feature-slug]/`
- Raise uncertainty rather than making an unconfirmed assumption
- Surface the feature slug to handoff agents — they need it to find context


### ⚠️ Ask first

- If the accepted idea references a technology or constraint you have not
seen in the project before
- If decomposition would produce more than 7 tasks (may indicate the
feature is too large and should itself be decomposed)
- If two AC items appear to contradict each other


### 🚫 Never do

- Write implementation detail into acceptance criteria
- Begin decomposition before both parallel tracks are confirmed complete
- Modify task definitions after handing off to Task Owner Agents
- Review your own feature spec or AC (that is QA Agent's role)
- Claim write authority over any file in `src/` — that belongs to Task
Performer Agents in Zone 4
- Accept a Product Owner decision on behalf of the Product Owner

```

***

Three structural decisions in the file worth noting:[^11_1][^11_2][^11_3]

**The handoffs encode the three real decision points** — not just "next step" navigation. The UI design handoff exists separately from the task owner handoff because they fire at different times and carry different context. A single generic "next" handoff would lose that.[^11_2]

**The workflow section is sequential with explicit gates.** Step 4 has a hard dual-gate that mirrors the policy P2.05 from the policy registry — both AC *and* UI artefact must be accepted before decomposition begins. Making this explicit in the workflow prevents the agent from rationalising past it.[^11_1]

**The project context section is concrete, not generic.** Because the agent file loads for every Feature Owner interaction, it's worth embedding the Astro/React distinction here — it will shape how every AC and decomposition gets written from the start, without needing to re-explain the stack each session.[^11_4]
<span style="display:none">[^11_5]</span>

<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^11_1]: event-storming-session-NbbGJS8yQ0.M4J0MHn24rw.md
[^11_2]: Custom_agents_in_VS_Code.md
[^11_3]: How-to-write-a-great-agents.md
[^11_4]: copilot-instructions.md
[^11_5]: SKILL.md

---

# note that the file isn't yaml compatible. description and handoff prompt need to be on the single line and tools need to be in an array. here is an example from the docs:

---
description: Generate an implementation plan for new features or refactoring existing code.
name: Planner
tools: ['fetch', 'githubRepo', 'search', 'usages']
model: ['Claude Opus 4.5', 'GPT-5.2']  # Tries models in order
handoffs:
  - label: Implement Plan
    agent: agent
    prompt: Implement the plan outlined above.
    send: false
---
# Planning instructions
You are in planning mode. Your task is to generate an implementation plan for a new feature or for refactoring existing code.
Don't make any code edits, just generate a plan.

The plan consists of a Markdown document that describes the implementation plan, including the following sections:

* Overview: A brief description of the feature or refactoring task.
* Requirements: A list of requirements for the feature or refactoring task.
* Implementation Steps: A detailed list of steps to implement the feature or refactoring task.
* Testing: A list of tests that need to be implemented to verify the feature or refactoring task.

Good catch. Here is the corrected frontmatter — the body content stays exactly the same as before, only the YAML block changes.

***

**File path:** `.github/agents/feature-owner.agent.md`

```markdown
---
name: Feature Owner
description: Defines features from accepted ideas, writes acceptance criteria, coordinates UI design, and decomposes features into tasks. Active in Zones 2–3. Invoke when you have an accepted idea ready to become a feature, or when a feature needs decomposing into tasks.
tools: ['read', 'fetch', 'search', 'new', 'edit']
model: ['claude-sonnet-4-5', 'gpt-4.1']
handoffs:
  - label: Hand off to Task Owner
    agent: task-owner
    prompt: Feature decomposition complete. Spawn one Task Owner Agent per task in the decomposition. Each Task Owner inherits the feature context package, acceptance criteria, and UI artefact attached above.
    send: false
  - label: Request UI Design
    agent: ui-design
    prompt: Feature accepted. UI design required before decomposition can begin. Feature spec and acceptance criteria are attached. Please design and prototype. Context curation for UI design should happen first.
    send: false
  - label: Escalate to Product Owner
    agent: product-owner
    prompt: Feature Owner requires Product Owner decision. Context and current state attached. Please review and advise.
    send: false
---

## Who I am
...
```

Two things to note for all future agent files:[^12_1]

- **`description`** must be a plain single-line string — no `>` block scalar
- **`tools`** must be a YAML inline array with single-quoted strings: `['tool-a', 'tool-b']`
- **`model`** can be an array too, which is worth using — VS Code tries each in order and falls back if one is unavailable[^12_1]
- **`prompt`** in each handoff is also a single line — no multiline blocks

Everything else in the frontmatter spec (name, handoffs structure, send, agent, label) is already correct format. The body markdown below the `---` closing delimiter is unrestricted and can use any markdown formatting freely.

<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^12_1]: Custom_agents_in_VS_Code.md


---

# here is my updated frontmatter to show the updated names of the tools, the correct naming for models:

```
---
name: Feature Owner
description: Defines features from accepted ideas, writes acceptance criteria, coordinates UI design, and decomposes features into tasks. Active in Zones 2–3. Invoke when you have an accepted idea ready to become a feature, or when a feature needs decomposing into tasks.
tools: [vscode/askQuestions, read/problems, read/readFile, edit/createDirectory, edit/createFile, edit/editFiles, search, web, 'sequentialthinking/*']
model: ['Claude Sonnet 4.6', 'GPT-5.3-Codex (copilot)']
handoffs:
  - label: Hand off to Task Owner
    agent: Task Owner
    prompt: Feature decomposition complete. Spawn one Task Owner Agent per task in the decomposition. Each Task Owner inherits the feature context package, acceptance criteria, and UI artefact attached above.
    send: false
  - label: Request UI Design
    agent: UI Designer
    prompt: Feature accepted. UI design required before decomposition can begin. Feature spec and acceptance criteria are attached. Please design and prototype. Context curation for UI design should happen first.
    send: false
  - label: Escalate to Product Owner
    agent: Product Owner
    prompt: Feature Owner requires Product Owner decision. Context and current state attached. Please review and advise.
    send: false
---
```

Now let's create the Task Owner spec

I have everything. Here is the Task Owner agent file.

***

**File path:** `.github/agents/task-owner.agent.md`

```markdown
---
name: Task Owner
description: Defines a single task from the feature decomposition — writes task spec, acceptance criteria, and declares required skills. Zone 3 only. Spawned by Feature Owner Agent after decomposition. One Task Owner Agent per task.
tools: ['vscode/askQuestions', 'read/problems', 'read/readFile', 'edit/createDirectory', 'edit/createFile', 'edit/editFiles', 'search', 'sequentialthinking/*']
model: ['Claude Sonnet 4.6', 'GPT-5.3-Codex (copilot)']
handoffs:
  - label: Hand off to Test Owner
    agent: Test Owner
    prompt: Task acceptance criteria defined and accepted. Please write the executable test suite for this task. Task spec and acceptance criteria are at the path provided above. Raise uncertainty if any criterion is too ambiguous to produce an explicit test.
    send: false
  - label: Escalate uncertainty to Feature Owner
    agent: Feature Owner
    prompt: Task Owner Agent has raised an uncertainty that cannot be resolved without feature-level context. Details and blocking question are in the uncertainty note attached above. Please advise before task definition can proceed.
    send: false
  - label: Escalate to Product Owner
    agent: Product Owner
    prompt: Task Owner Agent requires Product Owner decision. Context and blocking question attached. Please review and advise.
    send: false
---

## Who I am

I am the Task Owner Agent — a short-lived agent spawned by the Feature Owner Agent
at the moment of feature decomposition. I exist for the lifetime of exactly one task.

I am not persistent across tasks. One task, one Task Owner Agent.

I am hierarchical, not market-based. I do not claim tasks from a pool — I am
instantiated by the Feature Owner Agent and inherit feature context at birth. That
inherited context is already filtered, so I need less context than the Feature Owner
Agent to do my job.

---

## Zone scope

| Zone | My role |
|---|---|
| Zone 1–2 | No authority — read-only if context is needed |
| Zone 3 | **Primary write authority** — task spec, acceptance criteria, skills declaration |
| Zone 4+ | No write authority — once published, the task belongs to the Task Performer Agent |

---

## What I own (write authority)

- Task specification (`task-spec.md`)
- Task-level acceptance criteria (appended to task spec)
- Required skills declaration
- Task status transitions: `CREATED → ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA_DEFINED`

I do **not** own:
- The test suite (Test Owner Agent owns this)
- The context package (Context Agent owns this)
- Task status from `TESTS_WRITTEN` onward (write authority passes to other agents)
- The feature spec or feature acceptance criteria (read-only references for me)

---

## What I receive at birth

When spawned by the Feature Owner Agent, I am given:

- The feature spec (`.framework/features/[feature-slug]/feature-spec.md`)
- The feature acceptance criteria (within the feature spec)
- The UI artefact reference
- My task entry from the decomposition (`decomposition.md`) — one entry only

I use these as my primary read model. I do not re-request them. If they are missing
or obviously stale, I raise an uncertainty immediately before doing anything else.

---

## Commands I issue

| Command | Triggers event | When |
|---|---|---|
| `DefineTask` | `TaskDefined` | On instantiation, from decomposition entry |
| `ReviewTask` | `TaskReviewed` | Self-review before submitting AC |
| `DefineTaskAcceptanceCriteria` | `TaskAcceptanceCriteriaDefined` | After task spec is confirmed |
| `DeclareSkills` | `TaskSkillsDeclared` | Alongside or after acceptance criteria |

---

## Read models I require

| Read model | Required before | Where it comes from |
|---|---|---|
| Feature decomposition entry for this task | `DefineTask` | Feature Owner Agent (inherited at birth) |
| Feature acceptance criteria | `DefineTaskAcceptanceCriteria` | Feature spec (inherited at birth) |
| Feature context + UI artefact + feature tests | `DefineTaskAcceptanceCriteria` | Feature spec + UI artefact (inherited at birth) |

If any of these are missing, raise uncertainty to Feature Owner before proceeding.

---

## My workflow — step by step

### Step 1 — Load inherited context

On instantiation, confirm the following are present and readable:
- Feature spec
- My task entry in `decomposition.md`
- Feature acceptance criteria
- UI artefact reference

If anything is missing: raise uncertainty to Feature Owner immediately using the
→ Escalate uncertainty to Feature Owner handoff. Do not proceed without it.

### Step 2 — Define the task spec

Expand the task entry from the decomposition into a full task specification.
The task spec must answer these questions clearly:

- **What does this task deliver?** (one sentence, plain language)
- **What are its constraints?** (technical boundaries, out-of-scope items)
- **What does it depend on?** (other tasks, external systems, environment assumptions)
- **What skills does a Task Performer Agent need to execute it?**

Output format — save to:
`.framework/features/[feature-slug]/tasks/[task-slug]/task-spec.md`

```markdown
# Task: [Task Name]

**Parent feature:** [feature-slug]
**Task ID:** [task-n]
**Addresses AC:** [AC-n, AC-m from feature spec]

## Delivers
[One sentence]

## Constraints
- [constraint 1]
- [constraint 2]

## Dependencies
- [dependency or "none"]

## Required skills
- [skill 1]
- [skill 2]
```


### Step 3 — Define task-level acceptance criteria

Task-level AC translates the relevant feature-level AC items into task-specific
verifiable conditions. They must be:

- **Scoped to this task only** — do not reproduce feature-level AC verbatim
- **Written in plain language** — problem space, not solution space
- **Independently verifiable** — the Test Owner Agent must be able to derive
at least one explicit executable test from each criterion
- **Atomic** — one condition per criterion

Format each as:

```
TAC-[n]: Given [context specific to this task], when [action], then [observable outcome].
```

**Critical:** If you cannot write a task AC item that is unambiguous enough for the
Test Owner Agent to translate into a test, do **not** write a vague one. Raise an
uncertainty to the Product Owner instead — this is the earliest ambiguity checkpoint
in the system. A bad TAC here propagates through the entire zone 3–4 chain.

Append to task spec under `## Acceptance Criteria`.

### Step 4 — Declare required skills

Skills are the capability requirements a Task Performer Agent must possess to
execute this task. Be specific — vague skill declarations produce mismatched
Task Performer Agents.

For this Astro + React project, skills will typically include combinations of:

- `astro/components` — writing `.astro` component files
- `astro/content-collections` — working with content collections and Zod schemas
- `astro/routing` — file-based routing and dynamic routes
- `react/tsx` — React component development in TypeScript
- `react/islands` — understanding islands hydration directives
- `tailwind/v4` — Tailwind v4 with CSS custom properties (no config file)
- `shadcn/ui` — using and wrapping shadcn/ui primitives
- `typescript/strict` — TypeScript in strict mode
- `testing/vitest` — writing Vitest unit tests (once test runner is configured)

Append to task spec under `## Required Skills`.

### Step 5 — Self-review and hand off

Before handing off to Test Owner:

- Every TAC item must be unambiguous and independently verifiable
- Required skills must map to the actual work described in the task spec
- Task scope must not overlap with any other task in the decomposition (check
`decomposition.md` if uncertain)

Use the → Hand off to Test Owner handoff when satisfied. Pass the path to the
task spec — the Test Owner Agent reads from there.

---

## Policies I respond to

| Trigger event | My automatic response |
| :-- | :-- |
| `FeatureDecomposedToTasks` | I am spawned — begin Step 1 immediately |
| `TaskAcceptanceCriteriaRejected` | Revise AC — review the rejection reason, address it specifically, do not rewrite from scratch unless the rejection requires it |


---

## Uncertainty — when to raise it

Raise uncertainty (do not guess or invent) when:

- Inherited context is missing, incomplete, or contradicts itself
- A feature AC item is too broad to scope to a single task without making
an assumption the Feature Owner Agent has not confirmed
- The task entry in the decomposition describes work that cannot be made
independently testable (may indicate decomposition granularity problem)
- Two tasks in the decomposition appear to overlap — do not silently absorb
the overlap, surface it
- The required skills for this task depend on a tool or library not present
in the project (check `AGENTS.md` and `package.json`)

Format uncertainty notes as:

```markdown
## Uncertainty raised by Task Owner Agent
**Task:** [task-slug]
**What I cannot proceed without knowing:** [one sentence question]
**Why I need this:** [what decision it blocks]
**My best current assumption (do not act on this):** [for context only]
**Recommended action:** [what is needed from Feature Owner / Product Owner]
```

Save to `.framework/features/[feature-slug]/tasks/[task-slug]/uncertainty-[n].md`
and use the appropriate handoff to notify.

---

## This project context

This is an Astro 5 blog + React admin dashboard. When scoping tasks:

- **Blog-side tasks** will typically involve `.astro` components, content
collections, or static routing — scope accordingly; do not add client-side
interactivity unless the feature spec explicitly requires it
- **Dashboard-side tasks** will typically involve `.tsx` React components
hydrated with `client:only="react"` — scope to one component or one
data binding at a time
- **Styling tasks** use Tailwind v4 tokens from `src/styles/global.css` and
shadcn/ui primitives from `src/components/ui/` — never scope a task to
"add styles" generically; name the specific tokens or components involved
- **Never** scope a task to span both the blog surface and the dashboard
surface — these are different hydration contexts and should be separate tasks

File output paths for this project:

- Task specs: `.framework/features/[feature-slug]/tasks/[task-slug]/task-spec.md`
- Uncertainty notes: `.framework/features/[feature-slug]/tasks/[task-slug]/uncertainty-[n].md`

---

## Boundaries

### ✅ Always do

- Load and confirm inherited context before writing a single line of task spec
- Write TAC in problem-space language — no implementation detail
- Declare specific skills, not generic ones (`react/tsx` not `react`)
- Raise uncertainty rather than inventing an answer to an ambiguous scope question
- Self-review before handing off — every TAC must be independently verifiable
- Save all outputs to `.framework/features/[feature-slug]/tasks/[task-slug]/`


### ⚠️ Ask first

- If the task entry from the decomposition seems to encompass more than one
independently testable piece of work (may need re-decomposition)
- If a required skill maps to a dependency not in `package.json`
- If the task's scope touches a file that another task in the decomposition
also touches (potential write conflict in Zone 4)


### 🚫 Never do

- Write implementation instructions into the task spec or acceptance criteria
(that is the Task Performer Agent's job in Zone 4)
- Write tests (that is the Test Owner Agent's job)
- Modify the feature spec or feature acceptance criteria (read-only for me)
- Accept scope that spans more than one independently testable outcome — split
or escalate instead
- Proceed past Step 1 with missing or contradictory inherited context
- Review the Test Owner Agent's test suite (that is QA Agent Definition's job)

```

***

Two things to note for consistency with the Feature Owner file:[^13_1]

**`web` is omitted from tools** — the Task Owner Agent works exclusively from inherited context already in the repo. It should not be searching the web; that would be a context contamination risk. If it hits something it doesn't know, it raises uncertainty to the Feature Owner, not to the internet.[^13_1]

**`edit/createDirectory` and `edit/createFile` are included** — the Task Owner Agent needs to create the `tasks/[task-slug]/` directory and write `task-spec.md` into it. Without these it can only read, which isn't enough for Zone 3 write authority.[^13_2]


<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^13_1]: event-storming-session-NbbGJS8yQ0.M4J0MHn24rw.md
[^13_2]: Custom_agents_in_VS_Code.md

---

# i want to question the value of this. Does the agent need to know this?

```

I am the Task Owner Agent — a short-lived agent spawned by the Feature Owner Agent
at the moment of feature decomposition. I exist for the lifetime of exactly one task.

I am not persistent across tasks. One task, one Task Owner Agent.

I am hierarchical, not market-based. I do not claim tasks from a pool — I am
instantiated by the Feature Owner Agent and inherit feature context at birth. That
inherited context is already filtered, so I need less context than the Feature Owner
Agent to do my job.

```

You're right to challenge it. Let's think it through.

## What that section is actually doing

That prose is **documentation about the agent written for a human reader** — it explains the architectural decision behind the agent's design. It belongs in the agent spec markdown document (which we wrote earlier), not in the `.agent.md` file that loads into every session.[^14_1]

The agent doesn't need to know *why* it was designed hierarchically. It needs to know *what to do*. The distinction between hierarchical and market-based spawning is a framework design principle — it has zero bearing on the agent's behaviour in a session. It will never use that information to make a decision.

## The test for every line in an agent file

> **Will this sentence change what the agent does or doesn't do?**

If yes — keep it. If no — cut it.

Applied to those three paragraphs:

| Statement | Changes agent behaviour? | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| "short-lived agent spawned by Feature Owner Agent" | No — the agent is already in a session when this loads | ❌ Cut |
| "I exist for the lifetime of exactly one task" | No — it can't enforce or act on its own lifespan | ❌ Cut |
| "I am not persistent across tasks" | No | ❌ Cut |
| "I am hierarchical, not market-based" | No | ❌ Cut |
| "I do not claim tasks from a pool" | Borderline — but the Boundaries section already says "never claim additional tasks" in actionable form | ❌ Redundant |
| "I inherit feature context at birth" | Slightly useful — it tells the agent to look for inherited context rather than fetch it fresh | ✅ Worth one line |

## The replacement

The entire "Who I am" section collapses to a single actionable line in the **What I receive at birth** section, which already exists:

> *"I do not re-request inherited context. If it is missing, raise uncertainty immediately."*

That one sentence does the actual work. The three paragraphs of architectural prose do none.[^14_1]

**The rule going forward:** Agent files should read like an operating manual, not a biography. If a sentence sounds like it's explaining the design to a curious observer rather than instructing an executor, cut it.


<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^14_1]: How-to-write-a-great-agents.md

---

# are there any learnings we can take from the context engineering skills in this repo?

[https://github.com/muratcankoylan/Agent-Skills-for-Context-Engineering](https://github.com/muratcankoylan/Agent-Skills-for-Context-Engineering)

There are several direct and immediately applicable learnings here. Some confirm decisions you've already made; some reveal gaps worth addressing.

## Learnings That Confirm Existing Decisions

**Progressive disclosure is what you're already doing — but you may not be doing it rigorously enough.** The skill repo's design philosophy is explicit: agents load only names and descriptions at startup; full content loads only when a skill is activated.  This is exactly the argument for keeping agent files lean and moving capability into skills — but it also means the agent files you've written so far should be audited for content that belongs in a skill rather than always-on instructions. The "Who I am" content you just cut is a good example. The detailed workflow steps are another candidate.[^15_1]

**Filesystem-as-context is already baked into your design** — the `.framework/features/[feature-slug]/tasks/[task-slug]/` output path convention you established is exactly the `filesystem-context` skill pattern: offload agent outputs to files, use the filesystem as a shared memory layer between agents, enable dynamic context discovery without passing large payloads through handoffs.  This is load-bearing in your system. Every handoff between agents works because the next agent knows *where to look*, not because the previous agent *passed everything forward*.[^15_1]

***

## Learnings That Reveal Gaps

**Context degradation patterns are a direct threat to your framework — and you have no explicit defence against them.** The repo names four failure modes:[^15_1]

| Failure mode | What it means | Where it hits your system |
|---|---|---|
| Lost-in-the-middle | Information in the centre of a long context gets ignored | Task spec + AC + tests + UI artefact loaded together — the AC in the middle may be ignored by the Task Performer |
| Context poisoning | Incorrect information early in context corrupts all downstream reasoning | Stale feature spec inherited by Task Owner Agent |
| Context distraction | Irrelevant information dilutes attention from what matters | Agent files with architectural prose (what you just cut) |
| Context clash | Two pieces of context contradict each other | Feature AC and task AC written independently may diverge |

None of your agent files currently include an explicit instruction to detect and surface context clash. This is worth adding as a shared invariant in `copilot-instructions.md`: *if any two loaded context artefacts contradict each other, surface the clash immediately — do not attempt to resolve it silently.*

**The Context Agent has no compression strategy.** The `context-compression` skill exists precisely because curating context isn't just about what to include — it's about how to reduce it to the minimum high-signal tokens before handing it to the next agent.  Your Context Agent spec (not yet written) needs an explicit principle: *the goal of curation is the smallest possible set of tokens that gives the Task Performer everything it needs and nothing it doesn't.* Verbose context packages are not better than lean ones — they're worse.[^15_1]

**You have no evaluation layer.** The repo has dedicated `evaluation` and `advanced-evaluation` skills covering LLM-as-judge, direct scoring, and pairwise comparison.  Your QA Agent Execution is doing human-style task review, but there's no systematic framework for evaluating whether the QA Agent itself is making good decisions. In a play environment this doesn't matter yet — but as a future framework concern, the QA Agent's accept/reject decisions are the most consequential in the system and the least verified.[^15_1]

***

## One Structural Learning for Your Skills Folder

The repo's `SKILL.md` under 500 lines rule and the 3-level loading pattern (SKILL.md → MODULE.md → data files) is worth adopting explicitly:[^15_1]

```

.github/skills/
context-curation/
SKILL.md           ← trigger phrases, one-paragraph description, what to load
references/
compression-principles.md    ← loads only when needed
curation-checklist.md        ← loads only when needed
verification-rubric/
SKILL.md
references/
scoring-dimensions.md
uncertainty-protocol/
SKILL.md
references/
escalation-paths.md

```

Right now your skills are flat single files. The 3-level structure means the SKILL.md stays tiny (cheap to load always), and the detailed reference material only enters the context window when the task actually requires it. For a framework where context discipline is a core value, your own skill files should model that discipline.

***

## The Most Valuable Single Skill to Install

Of all the skills in the repo, `filesystem-context` is the one most directly relevant to what you're building right now.  Your entire inter-agent communication model depends on the filesystem working as a shared memory layer. Installing it — or extracting its principles into your own skill — would give every agent in your system a shared mental model of how to discover context, where to write outputs, and how to avoid the most common file-based coordination failures. It's the connective tissue your multi-agent system runs on.[^15_1]


<div align="center">⁂</div>

[^15_1]: ContextEngineeringSkills-README.md

---

# ok. so before we've even finished creating agent specs or building anything, I see the first two agent spec files have so much context included. The whole idea behind the system is to create agents that don't need so much context, because too much context leads to problems.

I've included the context and agent team I used to redesign an existing website on Monday. You can see how little context was provided and they did great work:

===

# ArtsiX Website, Agent Context Document
*Last updated: February 2026 | Phase 1: Public website update*


> This file is the single source of truth for all Copilot agents working on the ArtsiX website update.
> Read it in full before making any changes. Do not deviate from the rules defined here.


---


## 1. Project Overview


ArtsiX is a collaborative collective of traditional and digital artists creating human-made digital visual and sonic works. The website must reflect this, warm, cultural, premium, not a tech startup, not Web3-forward.


**Phase 1 scope (this build):**
- Public website update (all pages below)
- Commissions enquiry pathway
- Remove Storylinks entirely


**Phase 2 (deferred, do not build now):**
- Client login + Digital Artwork Streaming Portal
- Playlists, scheduling, screen/device pairing, analytics


---


## 2. Tech Stack


From `package.json` (pnpm monorepo):


| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Framework | React 18 + TypeScript |
| Build | Vite 7 |
| Routing | React Router DOM 6 |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS 3 + tailwind-merge + clsx |
| UI Primitives | Radix UI (full suite installed) |
| Animation | Framer Motion 12 |
| Forms | React Hook Form 7 + Zod + @hookform/resolvers |
| Form delivery | @formspree/react (env var: `VITE_FORMSPREE_FORM_ID`) |
| 3D (optional) | Three.js + @react-three/fiber + @react-three/drei |
| Icons | Lucide React + @iconify/react |
| Server | Express 5 |
| Testing | Vitest |
| Package manager | pnpm 10 |


**Note on suggested Next.js direction in brief:** The brief suggests Next.js + Sanity for a future CMS-driven rebuild. Phase 1 uses the current Vite/React stack. Flag to client when CMS integration is needed.


---


## 3. Brand Positioning


**One-liner:** "Human-created digital art, made collaboratively by traditional and digital artists."


**Tone:** Warm · Cultural · Confident · Plain-English · Premium but not corporate


**Avoid at all times:**
- Crypto / Web3 / NFT / blockchain / metaverse / token language anywhere on public pages
- Tech-startup framing ("revolutionising", "next-generation", "disruptive")
- Neon cyberpunk aesthetics
- Buzzwords: "magical", "visionary", "innovative", "ecosystem"
- Any mention of Storylinks


**Target audiences:**
- Primary: General public / art lovers; Hospitality & venue decision-makers (hotels, bars, lobbies); Curators / arts orgs
- Secondary: Collectors (light touch); Artists considering collaboration; Press / partners


---


## 4. Design System


### Colour Tokens (Tailwind)
```

Background page:    bg-neutral-950
Background cards:   bg-neutral-900
Headings:           text-neutral-100
Body text:          text-neutral-400
Accent (single):    text-amber-400 / border-amber-400
Borders:            border-neutral-800

```


### Typography
- Headings: Elegant serif via `font-serif` (configure in `tailwind.config.ts`)
- Body: Modern sans via `font-sans`
- Radii: `rounded-sm` or `rounded-none`, sharp, refined
- Spacing: Generous whitespace; breathing room is intentional


### Motion Rules (Framer Motion)
- ✅ Soft fades (`whileInView` with `opacity: 0 → 1`)
- ✅ Subtle parallax on hero section only
- ✅ Hover reveals: title + short descriptor on work/artist cards
- ❌ No aggressive transitions or rapid movement
- ❌ No parallax outside the hero
- ⚠️ Always respect `prefers-reduced-motion`, wrap all motion in a check


```tsx
// Required pattern for all animated components
const prefersReduced = window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches;
```


### Helper Utilities

Use `cn()` from `clsx` + `tailwind-merge` for all conditional class logic. Never use inline styles.

---

## 5. Navigation \& Routing

### Nav order (strict, do not reorder)

```
Home          /
Artists       /artists
Works         /works
Commissions   /commissions
About         /about
Human Creative Protocol   /hcp    ← always last
```


### Footer links

- Contact
- Instagram
- Privacy
- Acknowledgement of Country


### Routing file

```
All routes defined in a single central router file. Use `<Link>` (React Router) for all internal navigation, never bare `<a>` tags.
```


---

## 6. Page Specifications

### 6.1 Home (`/`)

**Goal:** Communicate "Human-Created Digital Art" within 5 seconds. Route users to Artists and Works in one click.

**Sections (in order):**

1. **Hero**, Fullscreen video loop (muted, autoplay, MP4/WebM with static poster fallback). Overlay: headline + 2 CTAs.
    - Headline: `"Human-Created Digital Art"`
    - Subhead: `"A collective of traditional and digital artists collaborating to create immersive visual and sonic works."`
    - CTA primary: `"View the Artists"` → `/artists`
    - CTA secondary: `"Explore the Work"` → `/works`
2. **What we create**, 3 cards: Visual · Sonic · Hybrid. Short plain-English description each.
3. **Featured Works**, 6 work tiles, lazy-loaded video thumbnails, hover reveals title + descriptor.
4. **Meet the Collective**, 8 artist portraits, `"Meet the Artists"` link → `/artists`.
5. **Commissions pitch**, `"We create bespoke digital artworks for venues, institutions and events, with visual and sonic options designed for each space."` + `"Enquire"` button → `/commissions`.
6. **Footer**, Acknowledgement of Country + socials.

**Mobile:** Sticky `"Enquire"` CTA button pinned to bottom of viewport.

---

### 6.2 Artists (`/artists`)

**Goal:** Browseable grid, fast filtering by practice type.

**Filter chips:** All · Traditional · Digital · Hybrid · Sonic
**Search:** Client-side name search (Fuse.js if available, otherwise simple `.filter()`)

**Artist card fields:**

- Portrait image
- Name
- Practice tags (chips)
- 1-line descriptor

**Implementation:** Client-side filtering only, no API calls. Tags are precomputed on the data object.

---

### 6.3 Artist Detail (`/artists/:slug`)

**Layout:**

1. Portrait + short bio (80–120 words, plain English, no buzzwords)
2. Practice tags
3. Featured works grid (links to `/works/:slug`)
4. CTA: `"Enquire about a commission"` → `/commissions`

---

### 6.4 Works (`/works`)

**Goal:** Showreel feeling without overwhelming. Fast filter.

**Filters:**

- Type: Visual · Sonic · Hybrid
- Availability: Available for licensing · Commissioned · Archived

**Implementation:** Lazy-loaded video thumbnails with poster frames. Masonry or equal-height grid.

---

### 6.5 Work Detail (`/works/:slug`)

**Layout:**

1. Hero video player, muted by default, user-controlled sound toggle
2. Short description (30–50 words, plain English)
3. Credits, linked to artist profile pages
4. Specs: loop length · year · format
5. Availability status + `"Enquire"` CTA → `/commissions`

---

### 6.6 Commissions (`/commissions`)

**Goal:** Make it easy for venues to say yes. This is the highest commercial priority page.

**Sections:**

1. **Who it's for**, Venues, institutions, events. Plain English. 2–3 sentences.
2. **What you get**, Bespoke visual/sonic/hybrid artworks, licensed for your space.
3. **How it works**, 3 numbered steps (e.g. Share your brief → We match artists → Work delivered).
4. **FAQ**, Accordion (Radix UI). Cover: screen formats, audio options, turnaround times.
5. **Enquiry form** (see spec below).

**Enquiry form fields (Zod schema):**

```ts
z.object({
  name: z.string().min(2),
  email: z.string().email(),
  organisation: z.string().optional(),
  interest: z.enum(['commission', 'licensing', 'collaboration', 'press']),
  message: z.string().min(20).max(1000),
})
```

**Form delivery:** `@formspree/react` using env var `VITE_FORMSPREE_FORM_ID`.
**Spam protection:** Honeypot field (hidden) + rate limit awareness.

---

### 6.7 About (`/about`)

**Sections:**

1. What is ArtsiX (collective, not a festival or startup)
2. How we work (collaboration + studio intensives)
3. Why it matters (human-led digital culture)

**No** blockchain, NFT, or metaverse language. Bios are person-first, practice-focused.

---

### 6.8 Human Creative Protocol (`/hcp`)

**Placement:** Last in nav. Not prominent on Home. No deep linking from other pages unless contextually appropriate.

**Format:**

- Short intro paragraph (3–5 sentences): What HCP means and why it matters to ArtsiX
- Accordion (Radix UI): "Why it exists" · "What we document" · "How it protects artists"
- No blockchain language unless a user explicitly expands an accordion and requests detail
- No icons, tech diagrams, or bullet grids, quiet typographic layout only

**Goal:** Credibility + ethics without alarming a non-technical audience.

---

## 7. Content Data Shape

Use these TypeScript interfaces for all data models. Wire to static JSON or a future CMS.

```ts
interface Artist {
  id: string;
  slug: string;
  name: string;
  portraitImage: string;       // URL or import path
  shortBio: string;            // 80–120 words
  practiceTags: ('traditional' | 'digital' | 'hybrid' | 'sonic')[];
  roles: string[];             // e.g. ['painter', 'composer']
  featuredWorkIds: string[];
  instagramUrl?: string;
}


interface Work {
  id: string;
  slug: string;
  title: string;
  coverVideo?: string;         // MP4/WebM URL preferred
  coverImage?: string;         // Poster fallback, required if video present
  typeTags: ('visual' | 'sonic' | 'hybrid')[];
  loopLength?: string;         // e.g. '3m 20s'
  shortDescription: string;   // 30–50 words
  creditIds: string[];         // Artist IDs
  year: number;
  availability: 'commissioned' | 'available-for-licensing' | 'archived';
  galleryAssets?: string[];
}
```


---

## 8. Functional Requirements

### Performance targets

- LCP < 2.5s on mobile
- CLS < 0.1
- INP: good


### Performance tactics

- Lazy-load all below-fold images and videos
- Preload hero poster image
- Use WebP/AVIF for images, WebM/MP4 for video
- Defer non-critical JS
- No unused Radix UI or Three.js imports on non-3D pages


### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)

- All interactive elements: `focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-amber-400`
- All images: meaningful `alt` text (not filename, not empty)
- All form fields: associated `<label>` elements
- Keyboard-navigable navigation and modals
- Captions or transcripts for sonic pieces where feasible


### SEO

- Schema.org: `Organization` on About, `CreativeWork` on Work Detail
- OpenGraph image per page
- Readable URLs: `/artists/artist-name`, `/works/work-title`
- `sitemap.xml` + `robots.txt`


### Analytics

- Plausible or GA4 (client to confirm which)
- Track: CTA clicks, enquiry form submissions, work detail views


### Security / Privacy

- Minimal cookies
- Privacy page in footer
- Form data routed securely via Formspree, no raw data stored in-repo
- No sensitive keys in committed code, use `.env` with `VITE_` prefix

---

## 9. Asset Requirements (Needed from Client)

The following must be requested before final build. Use tasteful placeholders until delivered.

- [ ] ArtsiX logo (SVG preferred)
- [ ] Brand palette confirmation (or propose 2 options)
- [ ] Artist portraits (min 1200px wide, square crop preferred)
- [ ] Work videos (MP4) + poster frames (JPG/WebP)
- [ ] Short artist bios (80–120 words each)
- [ ] Work credits and descriptions
- [ ] Acknowledgement of Country text
- [ ] Contact email address
- [ ] Instagram URL + any other socials
- [ ] Formspree form ID (or confirm alternative)

---

## 10. Copy Rules (Apply Everywhere)

| Replace | With |
| :-- | :-- |
| "revolutionising" | "changing", "shaping" |
| "next-generation" | remove or rewrite |
| "metaverse" | remove |
| "NFT" / "token" | remove |
| "blockchain" | remove from public pages |
| "Web3" | remove |
| "magical" | describe the work specifically |
| "visionary" | describe the person's practice |
| "ecosystem" | "collective", "network" |
| "Storylinks" | remove entirely, no references |


---

## 11. Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Home communicates what ArtsiX is within 5 seconds
- [ ] Artists and Works are reachable in 1 click from Home
- [ ] Filters work on mobile and desktop
- [ ] Commissions enquiry form delivers reliably and is spam-protected
- [ ] HCP page exists, is last in nav, is not prominent on Home
- [ ] No Web3 / NFT / blockchain / Storylinks language anywhere on public pages
- [ ] WCAG 2.2 AA passed
- [ ] Performance targets met (LCP, CLS, INP)
- [ ] All motion respects `prefers-reduced-motion`

---

## 12. Deliverables Checklist

- [ ] Live website deployed (all 8 routes)
- [ ] Style guide: typography, spacing, components, motion rules
- [ ] Content import template (JSON) for Artists and Works
- [ ] Admin guide (1 page) for updating Artists and Works
- [ ] CMS recommendation with rationale (Sanity vs MDX vs static JSON)

---

*Phase 2 (Digital Artwork Streaming Portal) is fully scoped in the brief but is NOT part of this build. Do not scaffold portal routes, auth, or streaming infrastructure in Phase 1.*
